topic.name: Problems on Trains
topic.description: Speed, distance, relative motion, and crossing problems involving trains, platforms, and poles. Estimated reading 25 min; practice 40 min.

theory.introduction: Problems on Trains apply the basic relation Distance = Speed × Time to moving objects of measurable length. Unlike a point object, a train has its own length, so the distance covered while crossing a pole, a person, another train, or a platform includes that length. Mastering unit conversion (km/h ↔ m/s) and relative speed for same/opposite directions is the core of this topic for competitive exams.
theory.explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

A train is treated as a line segment of known length. When it crosses a stationary point object (pole, tree, standing person), the distance equals the train’s length. When it crosses a platform, bridge, or tunnel of length L_p, the distance equals L_train + L_p. When two trains cross each other, distance equals the sum of their lengths.

Relative speed depends on direction: same direction → |S1 − S2|; opposite direction → S1 + S2. Always keep units consistent. Convert km/h to m/s by multiplying by 5/18, and m/s to km/h by multiplying by 18/5.

Time to cross = Distance / Relative speed. Speed = Distance / Time. Length (distance) = Speed × Time.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Railway timetable planning, estimating how long a train occupies a platform or level crossing, designing safe signalling distances, and logistics for freight trains sharing tracks all rely on these relative-motion ideas.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Forgetting to add platform length when a train crosses a platform.
2) Mixing km/h with metres and seconds without conversion.
3) Using sum of speeds when trains move in the same direction.
4) Treating a moving person like a pole without adjusting relative speed.
5) Using only one train’s length when two trains cross each other.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• A man/pole has negligible length → distance = length of train only.
• Platform/bridge has significant length → add both lengths.
• Same direction → subtract speeds; opposite → add speeds.
• If a train overtakes a man walking in the same direction, relative speed = train speed − man speed.
• If they move toward each other, relative speed = train speed + man speed.
• Always convert before dividing when mixed units appear.

formulas.title: Time to cross a pole or standing person
formulas.content: Time = Length of train / Speed of train

formulas.title: Time to cross a platform or bridge
formulas.content: Time = (Length of train + Length of platform) / Speed of train

formulas.title: Train crossing a man walking same direction
formulas.content: Time = Length of train / (Speed of train − Speed of man)

formulas.title: Train crossing a man walking opposite direction
formulas.content: Time = Length of train / (Speed of train + Speed of man)

shortcutTips.title: Convert first
shortcutTips.tip: If length is in metres and time in seconds, convert speed to m/s before dividing.

shortcutTips.title: Pole vs platform
shortcutTips.tip: Pole or tree → use train length only; platform or bridge → add both lengths.

shortcutTips.title: Same vs opposite
shortcutTips.tip: Same direction: subtract speeds; opposite direction: add speeds.

shortcutTips.title: Quick 5/18 check
shortcutTips.tip: Multiply km/h by 5 and divide by 18 to get m/s; reverse with 18/5.

shortcutTips.title: Overtake rule
shortcutTips.tip: Overtaking always uses relative speed as the difference of speeds.

shortcutTips.title: Two-train distance
shortcutTips.tip: Whenever two trains meet or cross, distance is always L1 + L2.

solvedExamples.problem: A train 150 m long runs at 54 km/h. How many seconds does it take to cross a standing pole?
solvedExamples.solution: The train takes 10 seconds to cross the pole.
steps: Distance to cross a pole = length of train = 150 m.

solvedExamples.problem: A 120 m train crosses a 180 m platform in 20 seconds. Find the speed of the train in km/h.
solvedExamples.solution: The speed of the train is 54 km/h.

solvedExamples.problem: Two trains 110 m and 90 m long run at 36 km/h and 54 km/h in opposite directions. In how many seconds do they cross each other?
solvedExamples.solution: They cross each other in 8 seconds.

solvedExamples.problem: A train 200 m long overtakes a man walking at 5 km/h in the same direction in 20 seconds. Find the speed of the train.
solvedExamples.solution: The speed of the train is 41 km/h.
steps: Let train speed be S km/h. Relative speed = (S − 5) km/h = (S − 5) × (5/18) m/s.
Distance = 200 m; Time = 20 s → relative speed = 200/20 = 10 m/s.

exercise.title: General Questions
exercise.description: MCQ practice set on Problems on Trains covering crossing poles, platforms, relative speed, and overtaking.

questions.question: A train 100 m long runs at 72 km/h. How long does it take to pass a telegraph pole?
questions.explanation: Speed = 72 × 5/18 = 20 m/s. Time = 100/20 = 5 seconds.

questions.question: Convert 90 km/h into m/s.
A 150 m train crosses a 250 m platform in 20 seconds. What is the speed of the train?
A train running at 36 km/h crosses a pole in 15 seconds. What is the length of the train?
Two trains of lengths 120 m and 80 m run at 40 km/h and 50 km/h in opposite directions. Time to cross each other is:
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 90 km/h = 25 m/s. Distance = 200 m. Time = 200/25 = 8 s.

questions.question: Two trains 100 m and 150 m long run at 45 km/h and 55 km/h in the same direction. How long will the faster train take to completely overtake the slower one?
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 10 km/h = 25/9 m/s. Distance = 250 m. Time = 250 ÷ (25/9) = 90 s.

questions.question: A train 180 m long crosses a man walking at 6 km/h in the same direction in 18 seconds. Speed of the train is:
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 180/18 = 10 m/s = 36 km/h. Train speed = 36 + 6 = 42 km/h.

questions.question: A train crosses a platform 200 m long in 30 seconds and a pole in 12 seconds. Length of the train is:
questions.explanation: Let length = L, speed = S. L/S = 12 and (L+200)/S = 30. Subtract: 200/S = 18 ⇒ S = 200/18. Then L = 12S = 120 m.

questions.question: A 240 m train running at 72 km/h crosses a bridge in 30 seconds. Length of the bridge is:
questions.explanation: Speed = 20 m/s. Distance in 30 s = 600 m. Bridge = 600 − 240 = 360 m.

questions.question: How many seconds will a 125 m train take to cross a man walking at 5 km/h in the opposite direction if the train’s speed is 40 km/h?
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 45 km/h = 12.5 m/s. Time = 125/12.5 = 10 seconds.

questions.question: A train covers 360 km in 4 hours. How much distance will it cover in 2 hours 30 minutes at the same speed?
questions.explanation: Speed = 360/4 = 90 km/h. Distance in 2.5 h = 90 × 2.5 = 225 km.

questions.question: Two trains start at the same time from stations A and B toward each other. They take 9 hours and 16 hours respectively to reach the opposite stations after meeting. If the first train’s speed is 64 km/h, the second train’s speed is:
questions.explanation: Speeds ratio after meeting times: S1:S2 = √t2 : √t1 = 4:3. So S2 = (3/4)×64 = 48 km/h.

questions.question: A train 110 m long passes a person walking in the same direction at 6 km/h in 11 seconds. Speed of the train is:
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 110/11 = 10 m/s = 36 km/h. Train speed = 36 + 6 = 42 km/h.

questions.question: A train passes two bridges of lengths 400 m and 300 m in 50 s and 40 s respectively. Length of the train is:
A 300 m train running at 90 km/h crosses another 200 m train running at 60 km/h in the same direction. Time taken is:
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 30 km/h = 25/3 m/s. Distance = 500 m. Time = 500 × 3/25 = 60 s.

questions.question: If a train 200 m long crosses a platform of equal length in 40 seconds, its speed is:
questions.explanation: Distance = 400 m. Speed = 400/40 = 10 m/s = 36 km/h.

questions.question: A train travelling at 60 km/h enters a tunnel 3 km long. How long will it take to completely clear the tunnel if the train itself is 0.5 km long?
questions.explanation: Distance = 3 + 0.5 = 3.5 km. Time = 3.5/60 hours = 3.5 minutes.

questions.question: The ratio of speeds of two trains is 3:4. If the second train covers 240 km in 4 hours, how much distance will the first train cover in 5 hours?
questions.explanation: Second train speed = 60 km/h. First = (3/4)×60 = 45 km/h. Distance in 5 h = 225 km.

questions.question: A train takes 10 seconds to cross a pole and 25 seconds to cross a 225 m platform. Speed of the train is:
questions.explanation: Extra 15 s are for 225 m ⇒ speed = 225/15 = 15 m/s = 54 km/h. Length = 15×10 = 150 m (consistent).

questions.question: Two trains of equal length take 10 seconds and 15 seconds to cross a telegraph post. If they run in opposite directions, time to cross each other is:
questions.explanation: Let length = L. Speeds L/10 and L/15. Relative = L(1/10+1/15)=L/6. Time for 2L = 2L/(L/6)=12 s.

questions.question: A goods train 350 m long passes a standing man in 28 seconds. How long will it take to pass a platform 210 m long?
A train running at 54 km/h takes 20 seconds to pass a platform. It also takes 12 seconds to pass a man walking at 6 km/h in the same direction. Length of the platform is:
questions.explanation: Relative speed vs man = 48 km/h = 40/3 m/s. Train length = (40/3)×12 = 160 m. Train speed = 15 m/s, so in 20 s it covers 300 m. Platform = 300 − 160 = 140 m.

questions.question: Without stopping, a train travels from A to B at 80 km/h. With stoppages it averages 64 km/h. How many minutes per hour does the train stop?
questions.explanation: In 1 hour with stops it covers 64 km, which takes 64/80 = 0.8 h = 48 min of running. Stoppage = 12 minutes per hour.

questions.question: A train 130 m long travelling at 45 km/h crosses another train of length 170 m travelling at 36 km/h in the opposite direction. Crossing time is:
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 81 km/h = 22.5 m/s. Distance = 300 m. Time = 300/22.5 = 13.33 s.

questions.question: A train crosses a stationary object in 9 seconds and a 250 m long platform in 24 seconds. What is the speed of the train in km/h?
questions.explanation: Extra 15 s cover 250 m ⇒ speed = 250/15 = 50/3 m/s ≈ 16.67 m/s = 60 km/h.
